segments.text:
Protein or DNA?
Properties of the Genetic Material
The ability to store genetic information, and allow access to/use of that information as needed
The ability to transfer genetic information accurately to daughter cells
The potential for heritable changes without substantial loss of information
Sufficient physical and chemical stability so that information is not lost or degraded over the lifespan of the organism

The accepted model for the structure of DNA,
early 20th century

Griffith’s “transformation” experiment (1920s)
Pneumococcus bacteria – cause pneumonia

heat-killed S-type bacteria resurrected?
non-virulent R-type bacteria transformed to virulent?

lysed S-type bacteria still
transformed R-type cells
to virulent
mouse not required

Avery, McLeod and McCarty (1940s)
follow-up to Griffith’s transformation experiments

Chargaff (1947)
new methods for extracting DNA intact
isolated DNA from wide range of organisms
DNA is long polymers/more than 4 nucleotides
not always 25% each nucleotide
Chargaff’s rule:
% A = % T and % C = % G
but % A does not always = % C

T2 bacteriophage
model system for Hershey-Chase experiment (1952)

label phage DNA with 32P
different culture
label phage proteins with 35S
let phage infect cells

after centrifugation – where are
radioactively tagged molecules?
32P-tagged DNA = primarily
with (inside) cells
35S-tagged proteins =
primarily in supernatant

fX 174 DNA alone
